desc:
This cave is shaped like a terrace with wide, stair-like ledges to the east and
west. Towards the west a cliff wall looms, so smooth it looks almost polished.
Eastwards, the cavern floor falls away again in another steep decline. A narrow
ledge leads out above the eastern chasm, following the southern cave wall in a
sharp bend, only to quickly disappear from sight.
name: Base of a Steep Cliff

desc:
This narrow, unsteady ledge clings to the rock face, painstakingly winding its
way southwards. To the west the ledge enters a cave, and footing is likely to be
more stable. The ledge itself is is littered with earth and pebbles. In places
there are gaps that must be stepped over, which further speak of the dangers of
loitering here.
name: Precarious Ledge

desc:
In places, this uneven ledge slopes downwards out from the rock face, and small
rocks and pebbles crunch underfoot: further heightening the risk of a fall. A
step in any direction seems hazardous and potentially fatal. At places the ledge
widens enough for easy passage, yet in other it is narrow enough that brushing
against the cave wall is inevitable.
name: Precarious Ledge

desc:
With a sharp twist, this tunnel bends toward the south and the east. The walls
are smooth and rounded, made of earth rather than hard rock. It is hard to say
if this is part of natural cave system or if this tunnel has been dug out.
Towards the east the tunnel open up into a wide space while it continues in a
slight downward slope to the south. The air is foul here, a stench of rot and
decay ever present as if something died and was left unburied.
name: Twisting Earth Tunnel

desc:
Droves of loose earth has fallen to the floor of this tunnel, making the footing
soft but taxing. The tunnel slopes down steeply toward the east to disappear
deeper into the ground. The tunnel seem much too rounded to be a creation of
nature, although there are no obvious sign of it being intentionally designed.
name: Earth Tunnel at the Top of a Slope

desc:
This serpentine, sloping earth tunnel winds its way down through layers of
densely packed earth. The tunnel seem to level out not far below this point,
although if that means that it is any closer to anything of relevance is
indeterminable. A long, wide groove runs along the centre of the tunnel, as if
something heavy was dragged along the floor recently.
name: Sloping Earth Tunnel

desc:
A deep furrow, almost a yard wide and half as deep, has been ploughed along this
tunnel. Something very heavy may have been either dragged or rolled through
here, going up the slope or coming down it. The walls hard-packed earth walls
are interspersed with small grey rocks of varying shapes.
name: Bottom of an Earth Tunnel

desc:
This long, narrow tunnel winds through hard, densely packed earth strewn with
rocks of varying sizes. Neither the tunnel's origin nor eventual destination is
obvious from this part of the passageway. To see beyond the next curved bend,
one would have to set out in either direction.
name: Narrow Earth Tunnel

desc:
A deep, narrow fissure follows the length of the ravine here, reminiscent of a
long, irregular wound of unknowable depth. Luckily, the fissure is too narrow to
fall down into, or the unwary could vanish forever in a moment of carelessness.
The ravine itself continues to the east and west.
name: Underground Ravine

desc:
A large boulder blocks most of the underground ravine here, firmly embedded in
the mix of hard earth and gravel underfoot. Passage through the area requires
squeezing oneself between the boulder and the rugged walls of the ravine. Many
smaller rocks lie scattered across the ground, hinting at a roof above that is
far from stable.
name: Underground Ravine

desc:
The great ravine through the deep subterranean bedrock comes to its end here,
the sides simply coming together to meet at a solid wall of rock. Where the
ravine is a bit wider, there is an area where the northern wall is considerably
less steep than elsewhere. A tunnel opening lies there, the opening a black hole
against the surrounding stone.
name: Underground Ravine by an Incline

desc:
A narrow cavern to the north leads out onto this open area. The tunnel ends at
the very brink of an underground ravine at least twenty feet across. The slope
down to the ravine's floor is steep, but rugged and uneven and should be easy
enough to traverse. A cold draft can be felt from below, slightly rustling some
old, dead cobwebs that cling to the limestone walls.
name: Brink above a Ravine

desc:
This low, wide cavern lies nestled in the very foundations of Arda, deep beneath
the world of men and elves. Erosion has hollowed out an uneven cavity with grey
limestone walls. Numerous stalagmites rise from the ground where slowly,
dripping mineral-enriched water has built up small, stunted formations over the
eons.
name: Eroded Limestone Cavern

desc:
A hollow cavity has formed here in the limestone bedrock. Layers in shades of
white and grey are easily distinguished on the walls. A narrow shaft, a natural
tunnel hollowed out through the ages, leads upwards here. To the west, a
limestone cave continues into the darkness.
name: Eroded Limestone Cavern

desc:
Here, a long, slim shaft has formed in the limestone bedrock. The walls are of a
stratiform of lightly grey and white layers of limestone. The various layers
have eroded in varying degrees, providing a multitude of small ledges to place
hands and feet on, making the shaft accessible to even the most inexperienced
climber.
name: Limestone Shaft

desc:
The walls of this grotto shift in streaks of white and grey limestone. The low
ceiling above could cause concern for the claustrophobic, and small stalactites
hanging down are a potential danger for the tall, unwary adventurer. A chute
near the southern wall leads down, deeper into the underworld.
name: Low Grotto

desc:
Towering stalagmites, ancient things built up through the ages by the slow
dripping of water filled with minerals, rise from the cavern floor towards a
ceiling lost in the darkness above. The cave is the size of a large cathedral,
with the walls to either side lost in gloom. Towards the eastern end the ceiling
is considerably lower than elsewhere in this massive cavern. A great crack runs
along the cavern floor near the centre of the area, and in places it is wide
enough for a horse to fall through.
name: Among Towering Stalagmites

desc:
The ceiling here is quite high, and many shelves, carved directly into the hard,
black rock, line the walls. Pots, kettles, tools and barrels lie half-cracked,
half-crushed all over the cave. Smashed chests, rusted iron tools and moulding
foodstuffs lie scattered in the dirt. Rats, roaches and centipedes scurry along
fissures in the ground. To the south a wooden door is slowly rotting and falling
apart; it is the only visible exit out of here.
name: Abandoned Reserve

desc:
The stone stairs turn quickly, leading upwards to another level of the
underground caves. A rotting, skewed wooden frame outlines the tunnel to the
north. A small hallway of some sort is located to the west. A tightly-packed
layer of dirt covers the ground, with deep footprints leading in all directions.
name: Inclining Staircase

desc:
Small openings to the north and west both appear to lead to large, dark halls.
To the east a smaller cavern can be seen, flickering with a faint light. The
pitch-black walls have a rough appearance, suggesting this hallway was quickly
excavated. The area is bare, except for some debris lying on the floor. Broken
tools lie spread in the hallway, along with smaller rocks, broken planks, live
cockroaches, and the bones from some sort of animal.
name: Narrow Hallway

desc:
Tall, rough black pillars extend down the centre of this hall, forming parallel
rows. The hall is wide and tall, its walls lined with sharp stone edges. Little
care was used when excavating the area: some of the pillars are crooked and
appear to be buckling under the weight of the mountain, and now and then ominous
creaking sounds can be heard. Small rocks and pebbles lie in the dirt around
some of the pillars. A big stone table rises from the ground: it has been carved
directly from the surrounding stone. A strong, putrid smell is ever-present in
the thick, humid air. The halls widen to the north, while to the south they
narrow into a smaller hallway.
name: Hewn Halls

desc:
This hall is in complete disarray: the innermost part has collapsed, leaving a
heap of rocks, planks, and other debris. A thick layer of stone dust covers the
whole area. Huge blocks of black stone lie cracked along the walls. The
dried-out husks of dead bugs and pulverized bones large enough to be from a
troll have been crushed by the rockfall. A small hallway leads eastwards.
name: Deteriorating Hall

desc:
This cavern is black as night and shaped like a funnel that opens up to the
north and closes off towards a pile of rubble to the south. There are a few
footprints on the ground, but because no flow of air reaches this forsaken
place, there is no way of telling how old the tracks are.
name: Funnel-shaped Cavern

desc:
The absence of sounds and smells gives this quiet, damp cavern an air of
desolation and abandonment. A fine dust has settled on the floor, where a line
of tracks tell a tale that the place is used at least intermittently. The roof
above is smooth, with just a handful of stalactites. A row of holes have been
hacked out of the wall in some far distant past, but it is impossible to tell
for what purpose.
name: Still Cavern

desc:
Here, a steady drip of water from above has turned the tunnel floor into a muddy
mush. Whereas the ground seems drier in the southern portion of the space, tiny
streams of water are flowing quietly towards the north. The cave walls are
black, with a greenish tinge along the bottom - quite possibly some sort of
lichen or mould.
name: Damp Tunnel

desc:
The tunnel floor tilts from south to north and from west to east. If it were not
for the gritty, mud-like gravel on the tunnel floor, passage here would be
slippery and perilous. Most of the water coming down from the cracks and
stalactites above gathers along the eastern wall, forming a slowly trickling
miniature stream that runs in a northerly direction.
name: Damp Tunnel

desc:
This tunnel slopes gently from north to south. A small stream of water is
trickling along the eastern wall, gathering up the water that drips down from
the dark, stalactite-ridden celiling above. A chopped-off torch mount has been
fastened to the western wall, but it is now too old and decrepit to use.
name: Damp Tunnel

desc:
The most notable feature of this otherwise non-remarkable cavern are the two
streams that meet here, one coming from the west and one from the south. The
streams, each just a small but steady trickle, meet here and disappear into a
black hole in lower northeastern corner of the cavern. There is a slight smell
of mould in the air, coupled with something faint but far more unsavory.
name: Confluence of Underground Streams

desc:
A rapid but quite small stream gushes out from a hole in the western wall. It
rushes across the gravelly tunnel floor, heading in an eastwardly direction.
Looking around the gravel reveals a number of dry patterns formed by the water,
indicating that the water flow varies with time. Higher and somewhat drier
ground can be reached by heading north. An odd, fungal smell hangs in the air.
name: Damp Tunnel

desc:
The cavern floor here forms a natural platform of sorts, with a couple of crude
stairs leading down towards the south. A rich, fungal smell hangs in the air, no
doubt emanating from the many layers of fungus and mould that blanket the lower
parts of the walls. There is a hole in the middle of the platform, about four by
four foot in size. A black, sooty patch can be seen in the north-eastern corner
of the platform, where ages ago someone lit a camp fire.
name: Smelly Cavern
